title: Docker Compose: Services unable to resolve each other by name
body: I'm running a multi-service application with Docker Compose. I have a `web` service and an `api` service. The `web` service tries to connect to the `api` service using `http://api:8000`, but I consistently get a `Name or service not known` error. Both services are defined in the same `docker-compose.yml` file. What could be the issue?

replies.body: This sounds like a common networking problem. Are both your services explicitly attached to the same custom network, or are you relying on the default bridge network created by Compose? If you're using a custom network, ensure both services list it under their `networks` section.

Try running `docker exec -it <web_container_id> ping api` from your host to check if the DNS resolution works from within the `web` container. This will help confirm if it's a DNS issue or something else.
Sometimes simply restarting Docker or doing a `docker-compose down` followed by `docker-compose up --build` can resolve strange networking quirks, especially if networks got corrupted.
Make sure there are no typos in your service names. Docker's internal DNS uses the service name directly. If your API service is named `my-api`, then `web` should connect to `http://my-api:8000`.
Can you share your `docker-compose.yml`? Without seeing it, it's hard to pinpoint exactly. The network definitions are crucial here.
Check your Docker daemon's DNS settings. If it's configured incorrectly, it can sometimes affect internal container DNS resolution, even for Compose services.
Ensure you're using a relatively recent version of Docker Compose. Older versions might have had different default networking behaviors or bugs that have since been fixed.
If you're using `links`, remember it's a legacy feature and not recommended for service-to-service communication. Service names should be enough for services on the same Compose network.

title: Difference between EXPOSE in Dockerfile and ports in docker-compose.yml?
body: I'm confused about `EXPOSE` in a Dockerfile versus the `ports` mapping in `docker-compose.yml`. What's the fundamental difference? When should I use one over the other, or both?

replies.body: `EXPOSE` in a Dockerfile is purely documentation. It tells users of the image which ports the application inside expects to listen on. It doesn't actually publish or open any ports on the host.
`ports` in `docker-compose.yml` (or `docker run -p`) actually publishes the container's port to the host machine. This is how you make a service accessible from outside the Docker network.
You typically use both. `EXPOSE` in the Dockerfile is good practice for clarity and for tools like `docker inspect`. `ports` in Compose is necessary for external access.
Think of `EXPOSE` as metadata. It's like a label saying 'this container has a service listening on X port internally'. `ports` is the actual operational mapping.

If you don't use `ports`, your services can still communicate internally within the Docker network (e.g., `web` to `api`) because they are on the same virtual network, even if no ports are exposed to the host.

Another way to look at it: `EXPOSE` affects internal container networking, making the port available to other containers on the same Docker network. `ports` affects external networking, mapping that internal port to one on the host machine.

An interesting nuance: if you use `docker run -P` (uppercase P), Docker will automatically map all `EXPOSE`d ports to random high ports on the host. So `EXPOSE` isn't entirely without function beyond documentation, but `ports` gives you explicit control.

title: How to create an isolated network for specific Docker Compose services?
body: I have a large `docker-compose.yml` file with many services. I want to create a separate, isolated network for a specific subset of services (e.g., `db`, `cache`, and `worker`) so they can only communicate with each other, and not directly with other services like `frontend` or `api`. How can I achieve this network isolation?

replies.body: You can define multiple custom networks in your `docker-compose.yml`. Define a `backend-net` and a `frontend-net`, then assign your `db`, `cache`, `worker` services to `backend-net`, and `frontend`, `api` to `frontend-net`.

To define a custom network, add a `networks` section at the top level of your `docker-compose.yml`, then under each service, specify which networks it belongs to. For example: `networks: - backend-net`.

Services not explicitly assigned to a network will go to the default bridge network. To truly isolate, ensure ALL services are assigned to a custom network, or that only the desired ones are on the shared network.
You can also have services on multiple networks. For instance, an `api` service could be on both `frontend-net` (to talk to frontend) and `backend-net` (to talk to db/cache).
This is a great practice for security and organization. It limits the blast radius if one part of your app is compromised and helps manage traffic flow.

Remember that once services are on separate networks, they can no longer resolve each other by service name unless they share a common network or you route traffic through an intermediary. Ensure your architecture supports this.
Here's a quick example: `networks: default: {} backend_network: {} services: db: networks: - backend_network worker: networks: - backend_network frontend: networks: - default`

title: Docker containers failing DNS resolution for external services
body: My Docker Compose services can communicate perfectly fine with each other, but they are consistently failing to resolve external hostnames, like `api.example.com` or even `archive.ubuntu.com` when I try to run `apt update`. I'm getting `Temporary failure in name resolution`. What could be causing this, and how can I fix it?

replies.body: This usually points to a problem with the DNS configuration Docker uses. Check the `/etc/resolv.conf` file inside one of your failing containers. It should point to a valid DNS server, typically Docker's internal DNS or your host's DNS.

Sometimes, the host's DNS configuration isn't properly picked up by the Docker daemon. You can explicitly configure DNS servers for Docker in `/etc/docker/daemon.json` (Linux) or through the Docker Desktop settings (Windows/macOS).
If you're on a corporate network, you might be behind a proxy or firewall that's blocking DNS queries or specific ports. Check with your network admin or try configuring proxy settings for Docker.
Ensure your host machine itself can resolve those external names. If your host can't, Docker containers won't be able to either.

If your host's `/etc/resolv.conf` has entries like `nameserver 127.0.0.53` (systemd-resolved), Docker might struggle. You can try setting Docker to use Google's DNS `daemon.json`: `{"dns": ["8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4"]}` and restart Docker.
Are you using any custom Docker networks that might have specific DNS configurations? Sometimes third-party network drivers or custom network setups can override defaults.
A quick test: `docker run --rm alpine nslookup google.com`. If this fails, it's a fundamental Docker DNS issue. If it works, the problem might be specific to your Compose setup.
Make sure no VPN client on your host is interfering with DNS routing, especially split-tunnel VPNs. They can sometimes reroute all DNS traffic.

title: Accessing a service running on the Docker host from within a container
body: I have a service (e.g., a database or a local API) running directly on my host machine at `localhost:3000`. I want my Docker container, which is part of a Docker Compose setup, to be able to connect to this host service. How can I configure my container or Compose file to achieve this?

replies.body: If you're using Docker Desktop (Mac/Windows), the easiest way is to use the special DNS name `host.docker.internal`. Your container can connect to `http://host.docker.internal:3000`.

For Linux, `host.docker.internal` doesn't work by default. You need to find your host machine's IP address on the Docker bridge network (e.g., `docker network inspect bridge` and look for Gateway IP) or its actual LAN IP.

Once you have the host's IP, you can use `extra_hosts` in your `docker-compose.yml` to map a hostname to that IP for your container. E.g., `extra_hosts: - "host.example.com:<HOST_IP>"` then connect to `http://host.example.com:3000`.

Alternatively, for Linux, you can try `network_mode: host` for your service in Compose. This makes the container share the host's network stack, allowing `localhost:3000` to work, but it sacrifices some isolation.
If using `network_mode: host`, be aware that all ports exposed by the container will be exposed directly on the host, which might lead to port conflicts and reduced security.
Also, ensure your host service is not listening only on `127.0.0.1` but rather on `0.0.0.0` or your host's actual network interface IP, so it's accessible from outside the host's loopback interface.
A simpler approach, if feasible, is to containerize the host service as well. This often simplifies networking issues within a Dockerized environment.
To find your host IP on Linux, you can run `ip -4 addr show docker0` (if `docker0` is your bridge interface) or `hostname -I | awk '{print $1}'` to get your primary IP and use that.
